query: management of cocaine-induced acute coronary syndrome in younger adults with uncontrolled hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine abuse is a major worldwide health problem. Patients with acute cocaine toxicity may require urgent treatment for tachycardia, dysrhythmia, hypertension, and coronary vasospasm in order to prevent pathological sequelae such as acute coronary syndrome, stroke, and death. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of cocaine toxicity and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of “unopposed alpha-stimulation,” in which blood pressure increases or coronary artery vasoconstriction worsens after the blockade of beta-2 vasodilation in cocaine-abusing patients, is controversial. This rarely-encountered adverse effect has resulted in some clinicians advocating for an absolute contraindication to the use of all beta-blockers, including specific, non-specific, and mixed. Many clinicians disregard this dogma and administer beta-blockers for cocaine-related chest pain and acute coronary syndrome, especially when there is demand ischemia from uncontrolled tachycardia. [8] Of the 1,744 total patients identified in the systematic review, only seven adverse events were from putative cases of “unopposed alpha-stimulation” due to propranolol (n=3), esmolol (n=3), and metoprolol (n=1). The authors of the original “unopposed alpha-stimulation” articles dating back to the 1980s concluded in a 2017 review that the phenomenon might be the from effects of cocaine alone, with or without beta-blockers. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: ACS is a manifestation of CHD (coronary heart disease) and usually a result of plaque disruption in coronary arteries (atherosclerosis). The common risk factors for the disease are smoking, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, male sex, physical inactivity, family obesity, and poor nutritional practices. Cocaine abuse can also lead to vasospasm. [4] [5] [6] A family history of early myocardial infarction (55 years of age) is also a high-risk factor.

retrieved_docs.title: Wellens Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although the Wellens pattern is highly specific for stenosis of the LAD coronary artery by an occlusive plaque, there exist mimics of Wellens syndrome or so-called “pseudo-Wellens syndrome.” Causes of this include cocaine use, in which coronary vasospasm can result in the typical Wellens ECG pattern. This usually resolves following the clearance of cocaine from the body, and the ECG ultimately returns to normal. It is important to recognize this and obtain an appropriate history, especially in a young patient with no risk factors for acute myocardial ischemia, as giving beta blockers could be detrimental due to potential unopposed stimulation of alpha receptors. In some case reports, marijuana use has also been shown to result in the Wellens pattern for unclear reasons. Another rare cause is myocardial bridging, in which the coronary artery tunnels under a bridge of myocardium resulting in LAD coronary artery stenosis. Additionally, the Wellens pattern can be seen in Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, which is thought be the result of myocardial edema.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Ectasia -- Etiology -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Some of the risk factors for coronary ectasia include: Hypertension Smoking Use of illicit drugs like cocaine

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Aortic Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patient education and deterrence should include the following: Smoking cessation advice Management of hypertension Avoiding the use of recreational drugs, especially cocaine Healthy lifestyle and diet Comprehensive patient education and lifestyle modifications are essential for preventing aortic syndromes and their complications.

retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, approximately 18 million individuals in the US live with CAD. The incidence is higher in men but converges between men and women after the age of 75. Additional risk factors include the following: Obesity Diabetes Hypertension High cholesterol Tobacco use (smoking history) Cocaine or amphetamine abuse Family history of cardiovascular disease Chronic kidney disease Human immunodeficiency virus infection Autoimmune disorders Anemia [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Crisis -- Etiology -- Substance use and withdrawal
retrieved_docs.content: Illicit drug use: Cocaine Phencyclidine Amphetamines and metamphetamines Excessive alcohol consumption, especially during binge drinking or withdrawal

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Hypertension -- Etiology -- Miscellaneous
retrieved_docs.content: Recreational drugs: cocaine, methamphetamine, MDMA, bath salts

retrieved_docs.title: Unstable Angina (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease affects a large portion of the population. It is estimated that coronary artery disease causes more than a third of deaths in people over the age of 35. It is the leading cause of death in this particular age group. Roughly 18 million within the United States alone are estimated to be affected by this disease. The incidence is higher in men, but as individuals surpass the age of 75, the incidence of males and females becomes much closer. Other risk factors include obesity, diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, smoking history, cocaine or amphetamine abuse, family history, chronic kidney disease, HIV, autoimmune disorders, and anemia. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine abuse is a major worldwide health problem. Patients with acute cocaine toxicity presenting to the emergency department (ED) may require urgent treatment for tachycardia, dysrhythmia, hypertension, and coronary vasospasm, leading to pathological sequelae such as acute coronary syndrome, stroke, and death. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of “unopposed alpha-stimulation,” in which blood pressure increases or coronary artery vasoconstriction worsens after blockade of beta-2 vasodilation in cocaine-abusing patients, is controversial. This rarely-encountered adverse effect has resulted in some clinicians advocating for an absolute contraindication of the use of all beta-blockers, including specific, non-specific, and mixed. Many clinicians disregard this dogma and administer beta-blockers for cocaine-related chest pain and acute coronary syndrome, especially when there is demand ischemia from uncontrolled tachycardia [4] . Of the 1744 total patients identified in the systematic review, only seven adverse events were from putative cases of “unopposed alpha-stimulation” due to propranolol (n=3), esmolol (n=3), and metoprolol (n=1). [8] The authors of the original “unopposed alpha-stimulation” articles dating back to the 1980s concluded in a 2017 review that the phenomenon might result from the effects of cocaine alone, with or without beta-blockers. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Hypertension management
retrieved_docs.content: Reducing alcohol consumption also has blood-pressure-lowering effects. Current recommendations are for men to drink no more than two drinks per day and for women to drink no more than one drink per day. This would help lower the systolic BP by approximately 4 mm Hg.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Preoperative Management -- Issues of Concern -- Drug use and Cigarette Smoking
retrieved_docs.content: Drug use is on the rise in the United States (US). Early exposure use may place a child at higher risk for escalating future drug use. Children that smoke tobacco or drink alcohol are up to 65 times more likely to use marijuana than children who never smoked or drank. Furthermore, children who used marijuana were 104 times more likely to use cocaine than their peers who never used marijuana. [7] As such, it is essential that we develop improved methods to communicate with children and adolescents concerning the risks associated with making poor decisions. Acute and chronic exposure to substances of recreational use, including cocaine, marijuana, alcohol, amphetamine, and marijuana, can have significant physiological effects on pediatrics and adult. Cocaine use may lead to myocardial ischemia and infarction by causing increased myocardial demand, accelerated atherosclerosis, thrombus formation, enhanced platelet aggregation, among other cardiac and pulmonary pathologies. These patients may present to the OR in an acutely intoxicated state with significant cardiopulmonary dysfunction. General and hemodynamic stabilization is critical in these patients. Heart rate, blood pressure, and myocardial status must be optimized and stabilized.

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathomimetic Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic use of sympathomimetic drugs like amphetamines or cocaine can induce end-organ damage due to persistent hypertension and vasoconstriction. Prolonged or excessive use of these agents may cause myocardial infarction, heart failure, cardiac arrhythmias, stroke, and arterial dissections. Intravenous use of these drugs places patients at risk of endocarditis and viral infections such as HIV or Hepatitis C if injection equipment is shared. Hyperthermia is a key predictor of poor prognosis in patients with acute sympathomimetic poisoning and may lead to systemic effects such as disseminated intravascular coagulopathy and organ failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: تعاطي الكوكائين (cocaine use) is clinically associated with زيادة ضغط الدم (elevated blood pressure). This connection indicates that individuals who use cocaine are at increased risk of developing hypertension, and clinicians should be vigilant in monitoring blood pressure in patients with cocaine use to manage potential cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use is clinically associated with both arterial erosion and increased blood pressure. The vascular damage manifested as arterial erosion reflects the harmful effects of cocaine on the arterial wall, while the stimulant properties of cocaine contribute to elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these interrelated complications is important for clinicians when evaluating patients who use cocaine, as they may require monitoring for vascular injury and hypertension and appropriate interventions to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with both unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and arterial dissection (تسلخ الشرايين). This interconnection indicates that exposure to cocaine can precipitate acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina as well as serious vascular injuries like arterial dissection. Recognizing cocaine use as a contributing factor is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of patients presenting with these cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain and cocaine use (تعاطة الكوكائين) are both clinically associated with malignant hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث). The occurrence of abdominal pain may serve as a clinical clue indicating the presence of malignant hypertension, while the use of cocaine is recognized as a precipitating factor that can trigger or exacerbate malignant hypertension. Recognizing these associations is important for clinicians to promptly identify severe hypertension in patients presenting with abdominal discomfort or a history of cocaine use, enabling timely intervention and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين), indicating that individuals who consume cocaine are at risk for damage to the arterial walls. This relationship highlights the vascular complications of cocaine consumption and underscores the importance of monitoring for arterial injury in patients with a history of cocaine use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with the development of unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), reflecting its potent cardiovascular toxicity. In addition, cocaine use is also clinically associated with abdominal pain (ألم بطني), indicating that its adverse effects can involve the gastrointestinal system as well as the heart. Recognizing both the cardiac and abdominal manifestations of cocaine use is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1655
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), indicating that individuals who use cocaine are at increased risk for this acute coronary syndrome. In addition, cocaine use is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين), reflecting the drug’s potential to damage the arterial wall. The vascular system (Vascular System) is identified as the anatomic site where arterial erosion occurs, linking the structural location of this pathology to the broader circulatory network. Together, these relationships underscore the significant cardiovascular hazards of cocaine consumption, including both functional disturbances such as unstable angina and structural injuries like arterial erosion within the vascular system, highlighting the importance of addressing cocaine use in patient care to prevent serious vascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2465
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with several serious conditions, including unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), anemia (فقر الدم), and arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين). The arterial damage caused by cocaine (تسلخ الشرايين) can contribute to the development of unstable angina, while the drug’s impact on the hematologic system can lead to anemia. Anemia (فقر الدم) itself is clinically associated with a feeling of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), indicating that patients who use cocaine may experience weakness as a symptom of underlying anemia. Recognizing these interrelated complications—cardiovascular instability, vascular injury, and hematologic deficiency—is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of individuals who use cocaine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) and unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) are closely linked to mental‑health and substance‑abuse screening/history, hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية), and frequently require cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). Unstable angina additionally correlates with cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين), essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي), and valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). Essential hypertension presents with elevated systolic, diastolic and mean arterial pressures, serves as the parent condition for hypertension with complications, secondary hypertension, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), rebound hypertension, primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and systolic/diastolic subtypes. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1535
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with several serious cardiovascular and gastrointestinal manifestations. It is linked to the development of unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and to elevations in blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم), reflecting its potent sympathomimetic effects on the cardiovascular system. Cocaine use is also associated with abdominal pain (ألم بطني), a symptom that can arise directly from the drug’s vasoconstrictive impact on visceral vessels. In turn, abdominal pain is clinically associated with pancreatic disorders that are not related to diabetes (pancreatic disorders (not diabetes)), indicating that cocaine‑induced abdominal discomfort may sometimes signal underlying pancreatic pathology. Recognizing the interconnected nature of cocaine use, unstable angina, hypertension, abdominal pain, and non‑diabetic pancreatic disorders is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of patients presenting with these overlapping clinical features.

Overall, the integrated model emphasizes the central role of primary hypertension and its vascular manifestations, the interplay between hypertension, myocardial infarction, unstable angina, and chest pain, and the influence of cocaine use on abdominal pain and blood pressure, while recognizing pancreatic disorders as an independent source of abdominal pain. These relationships are important for diagnosis, risk stratification, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 889
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion for substance‑related disorders, indicating that the presence of withdrawal symptoms from cocaine contributes to the classification of these disorders. Substance‑related disorders are organized under the broader category of withdrawal and relapse effects, and they include the drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) as one of their members. The drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) also encompasses specific treatment approaches such as maintenance therapy for opioid substances, reflecting the role of opioid maintenance programs in managing withdrawal. Within the overarching framework of withdrawal and relapse effects, rebound tachycardia is identified as a specific physiological manifestation that can occur during withdrawal or after relapse. This interconnected structure highlights how withdrawal phenomena, diagnostic criteria, syndromic classifications, therapeutic strategies, and acute cardiovascular responses are linked in the clinical understanding and management of substance‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders that are not diabetes are clinically associated with abdominal pain (ألم بطني). Abdominal pain, in turn, is linked to cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين), indicating that patients who use cocaine may present with abdominal discomfort. Cocaine use is also clinically associated with increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم), reflecting the hypertensive effect of the drug. Increased blood pressure is a recognized member of the myocardial infarction syndrome (اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب (SMQ)), highlighting hypertension as a key component in the pathophysiology of myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate a cascade in which non‑diabetic pancreatic disorders can lead to abdominal pain, which may be related to cocaine use, and cocaine use can elevate blood pressure, a condition that contributes to the development of myocardial infarction. Understanding these interconnections is important for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2247
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين) and with increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). Increased blood pressure is identified as a member of the cardiac muscle hypertrophy syndrome (اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب (SMQ)). Consequently, cocaine consumption can contribute to vascular injury and hypertension, both of which are relevant to the development or exacerbation of cardiac muscle hypertrophy. Recognizing these interconnections is important for assessing cardiovascular risk in patients who use cocaine.
